neighborhoods.name: Old Town / Chinatown
neighborhoods.character: Portland's Chinatown was the second-largest on the West Coast after San Francisco. Centered on Second Avenue and the surrounding blocks, it was a dense enclave of boarding houses, shops, temples, and association halls serving Chinese workers employed in canneries, railroads, and lumber. Beneath the streets, an extensive network of underground passages — the so-called 'Shanghai tunnels' — connected basements and provided shelter. Like all West Coast Chinatowns, Portland's was a bachelor society constrained by exclusion. The neighborhood also served as the Skid Road district for transient white laborers, creating an uncomfortable overlap of marginalized communities.
key_institutions: Kam Wah Chung & Co. (related outpost in John Day, OR)
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: Portland's Chinatown was the second-largest on the West Coast — if {ancestor_name} was Chinese and came to Oregon, Second Avenue was home base: a few blocks of Cantonese signs and familiar food in a city that made clear, through law and custom, that those few blocks were all that was on offer.

neighborhoods.name: Albina District
neighborhoods.character: Albina was Portland's Black community — the neighborhood where African Americans were steered by redlining, restrictive covenants, and real estate practices that kept the rest of Portland white. The district straddled the railroad yards of North Portland, centered on Williams and Mississippi Avenues. Albina had its own commercial district, churches, clubs, and a vibrant cultural life that existed because Portland's mainstream institutions excluded Black residents. The neighborhood was later devastated by freeway construction (I-5, I-405) and urban renewal that displaced hundreds of Black families.
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: Albina was where Portland allowed Black families to live — if {ancestor_name} was African American and arrived in Portland, the neighborhood along Williams Avenue was community and confinement at once, a few square miles of belonging in a city that preferred to pretend Black people did not exist.

neighborhoods.name: St. Johns
neighborhoods.character: St. Johns, on the peninsula where the Willamette meets the Columbia, was a working-class company town before Portland annexed it in 1915. The neighborhood served the lumber mills and the shipyards that lined the riverbanks. Frame houses on small lots, a compact downtown along Lombard Street, and the Cathedral Park under the St. Johns Bridge defined the neighborhood. During WWII, Kaiser shipyard workers swelled the population.
key_institutions: St. Johns commercial district on Lombard Street
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: St. Johns was the mill workers' neighborhood — if {ancestor_name} worked the sawmills or the Kaiser shipyards, the frame houses on the peninsula between the two rivers were affordable, close to work, and just far enough from downtown Portland to feel like a separate town.

neighborhoods.name: Vanport
primary_groups: WWII shipyard workers — Black and white
Southern migrants drawn by defense jobs
neighborhoods.character: Vanport was the largest wartime housing project in the United States — a city of 40,000 built on the Columbia River floodplain between Portland and Vancouver, Washington, to house Kaiser shipyard workers. It was Oregon's second-largest city. Unlike segregated Portland, Vanport was integrated by necessity if not by design — Black and white families lived in adjacent units. On Memorial Day 1948, a railroad dike failed and the Columbia River destroyed the entire city in less than an hour, displacing 18,500 residents. Many of the displaced Black families had nowhere to go — Portland's neighborhoods were closed to them by covenant and custom — and the disaster became a pivotal event in Portland's racial history.
key_institutions: Vanport Extension Center (later Portland State University)
Kaiser Company child care centers (innovative for the era)
neighborhoods.narrative_hook: Vanport was a city that existed for six years and then drowned — if {ancestor_name} came to Portland for shipyard work during the war, Vanport was likely home, and the Memorial Day flood of 1948 was likely the event that scattered the family into a Portland that did not want them.

major_employers.name: Lumber Mills and Sawmills
major_employers.wages: Sawmill workers earned $2-4/day (1900s-1920s), rising to $0.75-1.25/hr (1940s-1950s). Skilled sawyers earned premium wages. Lumber work was seasonal in the woods but year-round in the mills.
major_employers.working_conditions: Portland was built by lumber. The city sat at the intersection of the Columbia and Willamette Rivers, with the vast forests of the Pacific Northwest feeding logs downstream to Portland's mills. Sawmills lined both riverbanks. The work was loud, dangerous, and foundational to the city's economy. Missing fingers were a mill worker's badge of experience. The industry drew workers from Scandinavia, the Great Lakes timber region, and later Dust Bowl refugees looking for any work at all.
major_employers.narrative_hook: The sawmills made Portland — if {ancestor_name} worked lumber, the screaming saws and the smell of fresh-cut Douglas fir were the daily environment, and the missing fingertip or the bad knee was the price of earning a living from trees.

major_employers.name: Railroads (Northern Pacific, Union Pacific, Southern Pacific)
major_employers.wages: Section hands earned $1.50-2.50/day (1890s-1910s). Shop workers earned $2-4/day. Pullman porters earned low wages but tips could double income. Chinese laborers on railroad construction earned less than white workers.
major_employers.working_conditions: Portland was the western terminus of the Northern Pacific and a major hub for the Union Pacific and Southern Pacific. The railroad brought the city its role as Pacific Northwest commercial center. Chinese workers built much of the Oregon rail network in the 1870s-1880s. African American Pullman porters were among the earliest Black residents. The railroad yards and shops in the inner east side employed thousands in maintenance and repair.
major_employers.narrative_hook: The railroad made Portland a city instead of a lumber camp — if {ancestor_name} came as a Pullman porter or a section hand, the train that brought {pronoun_object} to Oregon was likely operated by the same company that signed the paycheck.

major_employers.name: Kaiser Shipyards (Portland/Vancouver)
major_employers.wages: Shipyard wages were excellent for the era: $1.00-1.20/hr for basic labor, $1.20-1.85/hr for skilled trades (1942-1945). Overtime was common and time-and-a-half applied. A shipyard worker could earn $200-300/month.
major_employers.working_conditions: Henry Kaiser's three Portland-area shipyards (Oregon Shipbuilding Corp, Kaiser Company Vancouver, and Swan Island) built Liberty ships and escort carriers at unprecedented speed. At peak production, the yards employed over 100,000 workers — many of them Black Southerners and Dust Bowl Okies recruited from across the country. Women made up a significant portion of the workforce. The shipyards were Portland's great social experiment — integrated workplaces in a segregated city.
major_employers.narrative_hook: The Kaiser shipyards offered {ancestor_name} wages that Oklahoma or Mississippi could never match — if {pronoun_subject} came to Portland for defense work, the shipyard paycheck was the lure, and the question of where to live in a city that did not want newcomers was the catch.

major_employers.name: Canneries (Columbia River Salmon)
major_employers.wages: Cannery workers earned $1-2/day (1880s-1900s). Chinese workers dominated the workforce through the 1900s. Work was intensely seasonal — concentrated during salmon runs.
major_employers.working_conditions: The Columbia River salmon canneries were among the largest employers of Chinese labor in Oregon. Chinese workers — called 'China gangs' — performed the repetitive, fast-paced work of cutting, cleaning, and canning fish. The invention of the 'Iron Chink' fish-cutting machine in 1903 was explicitly named for its intended purpose: replacing Chinese workers. Cannery work was cold, wet, and seasonal, with long hours during the salmon run.
major_employers.narrative_hook: The canneries needed fast hands and cheap labor — if {ancestor_name} was Chinese in Oregon, the salmon season meant sixteen-hour days on the cutting line, and the machine they built to replace {pronoun_object} was named with the slur that told the whole story.

major_employers.name: Bonneville Power Administration / Dam Construction
major_employers.wages: New Deal dam construction wages were good: $4-8/day (1930s). The Bonneville Dam project and Grand Coulee (upstream) provided thousands of Depression-era jobs.
major_employers.working_conditions: The construction of Bonneville Dam (1933-1938) on the Columbia River east of Portland was one of the great New Deal public works projects. The dam provided cheap hydroelectric power that attracted wartime industry — including the Kaiser shipyards and aluminum smelters. Construction workers came from across the Depression-ravaged country. Portland served as the administrative hub for the Bonneville Power Administration, which managed the region's hydroelectric system.
major_employers.narrative_hook: Bonneville Dam turned the Columbia River into electricity — if {ancestor_name} worked the dam construction, the New Deal paycheck pulled {pronoun_object} out of the Depression, and the cheap power the dam generated was what brought the Kaiser shipyards to Portland a few years later.

housing_patterns.initial_settlement: Portland's housing history reflects its waves of migration. Oregon Trail settlers built frame houses in the 1840s-1860s. Chinese workers were confined to Old Town boarding houses. Lumber workers lived in mill-adjacent frame housing. The WWII migration overwhelmed the housing stock entirely — Vanport was built because Portland simply had no room for 100,000 new workers. Dust Bowl refugees of the 1930s initially lived in auto camps and squatter settlements along the Columbia River.
housing_patterns.ethnic_clustering: Portland was one of the whitest major cities in America — by design. Oregon's original constitution (1857) barred Black residents entirely. Restrictive covenants and redlining confined the small Black community to Albina. Chinese residents were confined to Old Town/Chinatown. Japanese Americans were sent to internment camps in 1942 and lost their homes and businesses. Dust Bowl migrants settled wherever they could find space — east side neighborhoods, outlying areas, and eventually Vanport.
housing_patterns.trajectory: For white working-class families: rental housing near the mills or yards (1-3 years) to purchased frame house in St. Johns, southeast Portland, or other working-class neighborhoods (5-10 years). For Black families: extremely limited options — Albina rentals with few paths to ownership due to redlining. For Chinese families: Chinatown bachelor housing with no path to mainstream neighborhoods until the mid-20th century. The 1948 Vanport flood forced a crisis — displaced Black families had nowhere legal to go, which eventually cracked open Portland's residential segregation.

anti_immigrant_reception.description: Oregon was founded as a white utopia. The state's exclusion laws — barring Black settlers from 1844 to 1926 — were unique in the West. Chinese workers faced expulsion attempts, and Japanese Americans were interned. Portland's liberalism is a modern phenomenon; its history is one of the most racially exclusionary in the nation.
specific_incidents: Oregon Exclusion Laws (1844-1926) — barred Black settlers, with lash law enforcement until 1845 amendment
Chinese expulsion attempts (1886) — mobs attempted to drive Chinese residents from Portland and several Oregon towns
Japanese American internment (1942) — Portland's Japantown on the east side was emptied; most families never returned
Vanport Flood (1948) — 18,500 displaced, disproportionately Black, with no alternative housing available in segregated Portland
KKK dominance in 1920s Oregon — the Klan controlled the governor's office and much of the state legislature, targeting Catholics, Jews, and minorities
nativist_organizations: Ku Klux Klan (extremely powerful in 1920s Oregon — passed law banning Catholic schools)

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Allen Temple CME Church
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Membership records, baptism and marriage registers. One of the primary Black churches in Portland, serving the Albina community through segregation, the Vanport flood, urban renewal displacement, and gentrification. Records held by the church.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: Allen Temple was the anchor of Black Portland — if {ancestor_name} settled in Albina, the church was where community happened, because the rest of Portland made clear that community was not available anywhere else.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Chinese Consolidated Benevolent Association (Portland)
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Membership records organized by district association, including names, villages of origin, occupations. The Portland CCBA functioned similarly to San Francisco's, managing community affairs, mediating disputes, and organizing responses to anti-Chinese legislation. Records are fragmentary but held by community organizations.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: The CCBA was the unofficial government of Portland's Chinatown — if {ancestor_name} was Chinese in Portland, the association records may contain the village name and arrival date that American records never asked for.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: Portland Urban League
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Case files, employment referral records, housing assistance records. The Portland Urban League was founded in response to the WWII migration that brought thousands of Black workers to the city. The organization's files document the housing crisis, employment discrimination, and community formation of Black Portland in the postwar era.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: The Urban League was where Black Portland went for help when the shipyard jobs ended and the city wanted the workers to leave — if {ancestor_name} stayed in Portland after the war, the League's records may document the fight for housing and employment in a city that had recruited Black workers but made no plans for Black residents.

churches_and_mutual_aid.name: St. Stanislaus Polish Catholic Church
churches_and_mutual_aid.records_available: Baptism, marriage, and burial records in Polish and English. St. Stanislaus served Portland's Polish community in North Portland. Records held by the Archdiocese of Portland.
churches_and_mutual_aid.narrative_hook: If {ancestor_name} was Polish and came to Portland, St. Stanislaus on the north side kept the records — the sacramental registers in a language from home, documenting the family milestones in a city that otherwise took little notice of who came and went.

source_references.author: Marie Rose Wong
source_references.title: Sweet Cakes, Long Journey: The Chinatowns of Portland, Oregon
source_references.notes: Comprehensive history of Portland's Chinese community from the 1850s through the 20th century, covering the formation of Chinatown, anti-Chinese agitation, the bachelor society, and the community's persistence through exclusion. Essential source for Chinese life in the Pacific Northwest.

source_references.author: Manly Maben
source_references.title: Vanport
source_references.notes: The definitive account of Vanport — its construction, its six years as Oregon's second-largest city, and the 1948 flood that destroyed it. Documents the social experiment of wartime housing, the racial dynamics of an integrated city within a segregated region, and the displacement crisis that followed the flood.

source_references.author: William Toll
source_references.title: The Making of an Ethnic Middle Class: Portland Jewry over Four Generations
source_references.notes: While focused on Portland's Jewish community, Toll's study provides a framework for understanding ethnic community formation in Portland — a city small enough that immigrant communities were intimate and interconnected, and exclusionary enough that self-contained institutions were necessary for survival.

source_references.author: Quintard Taylor
source_references.title: The Forging of a Black Community: Seattle's Central District from 1870 through the Civil Rights Era
source_references.notes: While focused on Seattle, Taylor's study of Black community formation in the Pacific Northwest provides essential comparative context for understanding Portland's Albina district. The regional patterns of Black exclusion and community building were shared across the Pacific Northwest.
